The current pipeline is selecting the wrong source or wrong resolver for several low-performing attributes: genres uses most_complete even though validation expects complementary genres combined from goodreads+metabooks; publisher uses most_complete even though source-vs-validation shows metabooks is exact 100% and should be trusted; title and page_count trust maps are reversed, currently favoring amazon although amazon is the weakest source in the probe data. There is also a likely source-loading mismatch in code: the pipeline loads normalization/attempt_1 while the current normalized files are in attempt_3, which may explain some regressions, but under the routing rules the next fixable stage is fusion logic because blocking/matching are frozen-good and the dominant configurable errors are resolver/trust choices.",

evaluation_reasoning_brief.root_cause: The main remaining errors are now in fusion strategy, not normalization, blocking, or matching. Blocking/matching are healthy (all correspondence files non-empty, F1 ~0.80-0.85, no recall issues), and the prior normalization regression on isbn_clean is already fixed/protected at 100%. The current pipeline is selecting the wrong source or wrong resolver for several low-performing attributes: genres uses most_complete even though validation expects complementary genres combined from goodreads+metabooks; publisher uses most_complete even though source-vs-validation shows metabooks is exact 100% and should be trusted; title and page_count trust maps are reversed, currently favoring amazon although amazon is the weakest source in the probe data. There is also a likely source-loading mismatch in code: the pipeline loads normalization/attempt_1 while the current normalized files are in attempt_3, which may explain some regressions, but under the routing rules the next fixable stage is fusion logic because blocking/matching are frozen-good and the dominant configurable errors are resolver/trust choices.
evaluation_reasoning_brief.next_step: Route to pipeline_adaption because the highest-impact remaining fixes are attribute fusers and trust maps. Do not touch protected attributes already >85%, especially publish_year and isbn_clean at 100%. genres is the top improvable attribute and needs union-style fusion to match validation provenance patterns like goodreads+metabooks. publisher, title, and page_count all show wrong source preference under current trust ordering. Although the code appears to load attempt_1 normalized files instead of attempt_3, the actionable next stage within the allowed routing is still fusion adaptation: change resolvers/trust maps and preserve working attributes. Normalization should not be revisited first because current mismatch examples for the priority attributes are mostly wrong value choice, not simple formatting, and isbn_clean is no longer broken.
evaluation_reasoning_brief.takeaway: The main remaining errors are now in fusion strategy, not normalization, blocking, or matching. Blocking/matching are healthy (all correspondence files non-empty, F1 ~0.80-0.85, no recall issues), and the prior normalization regression on isbn_clean is already fixed/protected at 100%. The current pipeline is selecting the wrong source or wrong resolver for several low-performing attributes: genres uses most_complete even though validation expects complementary genres combined from goodreads+metabooks; publisher uses most_complete even though source-vs-validation shows metabooks is exact 100% and should be trusted; title and page_count trust maps are reversed, currently favoring amazon although amazon is the weakest source in the probe data. There is also a likely source-loading mismatch in code: the pipeline loads normalization/attempt_1 while the current normalized files are in attempt_3, which may explain some regressions, but under the routing rules the next fixable stage is fusion logic because blocking/matching are frozen-good and the dominant configurable errors are resolver/trust choices.

recommendations.issue: Current resolver most_complete picks a single source's longer genre string, but validation expects complementary genres combined across sources, typically goodreads_small + metabooks_small. This causes systematic under-recall and occasional missing values.
recommendations.fix: Change genres fuser from most_complete to union.
recommendations.resolver_reasoning: Validation samples explicitly show combined provenance (e.g. goodreads+metabooks), and mismatch examples show fused values are often strict subsets of expected values. Union best matches list-like complementary data. Voting would keep only shared/common values and lose recall; intersection would be even worse; prefer_higher_trust/favour_sources would choose one source and miss complementary genres. Because genres accuracy is only 30% and classified improvable with 100% ceiling, this is the clearest fusion fix.

recommendations.issue: Current resolver most_complete chooses verbose/imprint variants like 'Simon & Schuster (Trade Division)' or wrong imprints like 'Eos', while validation usually wants the canonical publisher name. Probe data shows metabooks_small matches validation exactly in 100% of examined cases.
recommendations.fix: Change publisher fuser from most_complete to prefer_higher_trust with trust_map {'metabooks_small': 3.0, 'goodreads_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
recommendations.resolver_reasoning: Source-vs-validation evidence is decisive: metabooks_small exact=100%, goodreads_small exact=67%, amazon_small exact=20%. prefer_higher_trust is therefore the best resolver. most_complete over-selects noisy longer publisher strings; longest_string would worsen that; voting can fail when amazon/goodreads agree on a non-canonical imprint. A strong trust preference for metabooks aligns with the validation provenance examples.

recommendations.issue: Current title fuser prefer_higher_trust is configured backwards, favoring amazon_small, but probe data shows goodreads_small is the strongest source and amazon_small the weakest. This yields expanded/marketing titles instead of validation's canonical titles.
recommendations.fix: Keep prefer_higher_trust for title but reverse trust_map to {'goodreads_small': 3.0, 'metabooks_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
recommendations.resolver_reasoning: Probe comparison: goodreads_small exact=67%, metabooks_small exact=50%, amazon_small exact=30%, with fuzzy 100% for goodreads/metabooks. Validation often prefers canonical shorter title forms from goodreads, not amazon's embellished variants. shortest_string is tempting for some cases but would be brittle and could truncate legitimate parenthetical titles that validation keeps. prefer_higher_trust with corrected ordering best matches observed source quality while preserving existing behavior style.

recommendations.issue: Current page_count fuser prefer_higher_trust is also reversed, favoring amazon_small even though amazon_small has 0% exact and 0% non-null in the probe sample. Validation often matches metabooks_small for page_count provenance.
recommendations.fix: Keep prefer_higher_trust for page_count but reverse trust_map to {'metabooks_small': 3.0, 'goodreads_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
recommendations.resolver_reasoning: The evidence shows amazon_small is the worst source here, so current trust is actively harmful. maximum/median are not appropriate because page counts differ by edition and arithmetic aggregation can create values no source contains. voting is weak with only 2-3 sources and frequent disagreements. prefer_higher_trust toward metabooks is the safest improvement, though this attribute remains structurally limited.

recommendations.issue: Language has regressed to 80% due to missing fused values, but it is near-protected and should not be over-tuned aggressively.
recommendations.fix: Preserve current resolver for language for now; if revisited later, test most_complete or prefer_higher_trust with metabooks-first only if missingness persists after broader fusion fixes.
recommendations.resolver_reasoning: Language is already relatively strong and not among the top-impact attributes. Unnecessary changes risk regressing a mostly working field. The current priority is genres/publisher/title/page_count.

evaluation_reasoning_brief.ceiling_assessment: With current matching/blocking structure, overall accuracy around 0.80-0.85 appears realistic. The supplied ceiling estimate is 88.5%, but page_count/publisher/title are partly structurally limited by source disagreement, so a practical near-term target after fusion fixes is low 80s rather than perfect accuracy.

normalization_report.llm_reasoning: Target style keeps title/author/language/publisher in original mixed case, stores publish_year and page_count as integers, and genres as a comma-separated string rather than a list object. IDs and isbn_clean were preserved per rules. To improve the low-accuracy fields, only minimal visible fixes were applied: cast numeric float years/pages to int, trim text whitespace, and title-case metabooks publisher because source shows inconsistent casing like 'Troll Communications Llc' and 'HarperCollins' while target publisher values are conventional title-style names.

investigator_action_plan.action: Change genres fuser from most_complete to union.
Change publisher fuser from most_complete to prefer_higher_trust with trust_map {'metabooks_small': 3.0, 'goodreads_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
Keep prefer_higher_trust for title but reverse trust_map to {'goodreads_small': 3.0, 'metabooks_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
Keep prefer_higher_trust for page_count but reverse trust_map to {'metabooks_small': 3.0, 'goodreads_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
Preserve current resolver for language for now; if revisited later, test most_complete or prefer_higher_trust with metabooks-first only if missingness persists after broader fusion fixes.

agent_run_summary.agent_loop_overview: The agent profiles the datasets, optionally reruns normalization, keeps the tested blocking setup, searches or refreshes matching when needed, generates a fusion pipeline, generates and runs an evaluation script, diagnoses the result, and then routes either back to matching, back to normalization, back to pipeline adaptation, or to final human review.
agent_run_summary.final_route: finish automatic passes and export for human review
agent_run_summary.next_step_advice: Route to pipeline_adaption because the highest-impact remaining fixes are attribute fusers and trust maps. Do not touch protected attributes already >85%, especially publish_year and isbn_clean at 100%. genres is the top improvable attribute and needs union-style fusion to match validation provenance patterns like goodreads+metabooks. publisher, title, and page_count all show wrong source preference under current trust ordering. Although the code appears to load attempt_1 normalized files instead of attempt_3, the actionable next stage within the allowed routing is still fusion adaptation: change resolvers/trust maps and preserve working attributes. Normalization should not be revisited first because current mismatch examples for the priority attributes are mostly wrong value choice, not simple formatting, and isbn_clean is no longer broken.
agent_run_summary.report_takeaway: The main remaining errors are now in fusion strategy, not normalization, blocking, or matching. Blocking/matching are healthy (all correspondence files non-empty, F1 ~0.80-0.85, no recall issues), and the prior normalization regression on isbn_clean is already fixed/protected at 100%. The current pipeline is selecting the wrong source or wrong resolver for several low-performing attributes: genres uses most_complete even though validation expects complementary genres combined from goodreads+metabooks; publisher uses most_complete even though source-vs-validation shows metabooks is exact 100% and should be trusted; title and page_count trust maps are reversed, currently favoring amazon although amazon is the weakest source in the probe data. There is also a likely source-loading mismatch in code: the pipeline loads normalization/attempt_1 while the current normalized files are in attempt_3, which may explain some regressions, but under the routing rules the next fixable stage is fusion logic because blocking/matching are frozen-good and the dominant configurable errors are resolver/trust choices.
top_planned_actions: Change genres fuser from most_complete to union.
Change publisher fuser from most_complete to prefer_higher_trust with trust_map {'metabooks_small': 3.0, 'goodreads_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.
Keep prefer_higher_trust for title but reverse trust_map to {'goodreads_small': 3.0, 'metabooks_small': 2.0, 'amazon_small': 1.0}.

Register how to fuse an attribute (unified API).
Accepts either a pre-built AttributeValueFuser (advanced usage) or a
resolver function (simple usage). When a resolver function is provided,
it is wrapped into an AttributeValueFuser with any extra keyword args
supplied via
fuser_kwargs
(e.g., accessor=…, required=True).
Parameters
:
attribute
(
str
) – Name of the attribute to fuse.

AttributeValueFuser
,
ConflictResolutionFunction
]

resolves conflicts and returns (value, confidence, metadata).
evaluation_function

]

Source code for PyDI.informationextraction.evaluation
"""Evaluation framework for information extraction in PyDI.
This module provides an evaluator to compare extracted (predicted)
attributes against an expected/validation dataset with common IR-style metrics.
It supports per-attribute evaluation functions (e.g., exact match,
tokenized string match, numeric tolerance) and produces both attribute-level
and dataset-level (micro/macro) metrics, including precision, recall, F1, and
non-null accuracy.
"""

annotations

,

,

,


PyDI.io.loaders module

Provenance-aware pandas I/O wrappers.
This module provides thin wrappers around common pandas readers that:
Add a unique identifier column to each loaded DataFrame
Attach detailed provenance metadata to
df.attrs["provenance"]
Optionally include minimal provenance columns (
__source_path
,
__dataset_name
)
The functions follow the design guidelines in
CLAUDE.md
: pandas-first,
rich NumPy-style docstrings, and lightweight logging for observability.

PyDI.fusion.evaluation module


This module provides tools for evaluating the quality of fusion results
against validation/test datasets.


Provenance-aware I/O helpers for PyDI.
This package exposes convenience wrappers around pandas readers that attach
provenance metadata and add a unique id column.
